title: A 3D Simulation Framework for Behavior Cloning  on Edge AI-Enabled E-Scooters in Smart Cities
author: Artemis, Stefanidou
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This work presents a 3D simulation framework for training and evaluating Behavior 
 Cloning (BC) models on edge AI-enabled e-scooters. The proposed approach uses binary vi- sual input and lightweight neural policies to enable autonomous navigation under constrained 
 computational resources. Using the CARLA simulator, a dataset was collected in Town01 with four discrete control actions (forward, left, right, stop), ensuring balanced maneuver representation. Several visual backbones were explored, including ResNet50, MobileNetV3, and DINOv2, combined with MLP and XGBoost policy heads. Experimental results show that the ResNet50+MLP configuration achieved the highest performance (99.6% accuracy, 0.996 weighted F1) while maintaining real-time inference (140 FPS) with only 94 MB of model size. These findings demonstrate that reliable visual imitation can be achieved even with simplified inputs and modest hardware, enabling practical deployment of autonomous or teleoperated e-scooters within smart city environments.
steps_raw:
This work presents a 3D simulation framework for training and evaluating Behavior 
 Cloning (BC) models on edge AI-enabled e-scooters. The proposed approach uses binary vi- sual input and lightweight neural policies to enable autonomous navigation under constrained 
 computational resources. Using the CARLA simulator, a dataset was collected in Town01 with four discrete control actions (forward, left, right, stop), ensuring balanced maneuver representation. Several visual backbones were explored, including ResNet50, MobileNetV3, and DINOv2, combined with MLP and XGBoost policy heads. Experimental results show that the ResNet50+MLP configuration achieved the highest performance (99.6% accuracy, 0.996 weighted F1) while maintaining real-time inference (140 FPS) with only 94 MB of model size. These findings demonstrate that reliable visual imitation can be achieved even with simplified inputs and modest hardware, enabling practical deployment of autonomous or teleoperated e-scooters within smart city environments.
